context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Participatory Development

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What are the ways in which participation can be achieved? What's the measure of participation and how must one judge it? Can the tension between participation as a goal and as a method be resolved and if so how? What were the structural factors that made participation such a key element in development strategies?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hi, i’m stuck on a 700–800 word reflection abt participatory development. i kinda get the topic, but idk how to answer the parts abt measuring participation and the goal vs method tension.
exchanges.tutor: Good, you've identified the parts that are tricky — that's a solid start. Before we dig into those, what do you already understand about participation in development? What does it mean to you in this context?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified two specific parts of the prompt they're struggling with. Rather than scaffolding all parts at once, I should start by probing what they already know to build from their existing understanding. I'll ask them to share what they do know first, which helps me gauge where to guide them and activates prior knowledge before tackling the harder parts.

exchanges.student: i think it means ppl affected by a project actually having some say in it, not just being told what’s happening. but ngl i’m not sure if “say” means just being consulted or actually sharing power.
exchanges.tutor: That distinction you just made — consultation vs. power-sharing — is actually really important and gets at the heart of the measurement question. Think about it: if those are two ends of a spectrum, what might sit in between? Can you think of any intermediate stages?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually identified a really important distinction on their own — the difference between consultation and power-sharing. This is directly relevant to the measurement question and the goal vs. method tension. I should affirm this insight and push them to develop it further, as it's essentially the ladder of participation concept without them knowing it. I'll ask them to think about what the spectrum between those two extremes might look like, which will help them build toward answering the 'measure of participation' part of the prompt.

exchanges.student: maybe like, first ppl get informed, then consulted, then maybe they help decide some stuff? and then the strongest version is they control it?
exchanges.tutor: You've basically built a framework there yourself — nice. So if that spectrum exists, how could it help you answer the question about *measuring* participation? What would 'more' or 'less' participation actually look like using what you just described?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has essentially reconstructed Arnstein's ladder of participation on their own, which is excellent. I should affirm this and connect it back to the measurement question in the prompt without giving the answer away. This is a good moment to push them to think about how this spectrum they've described could serve as a measuring tool.

exchanges.student: i guess “more” participation = more actual influence over decisions, not just being present at meetings. is that too simple, or is that basically the point?
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid core idea, not too simple at all. But here's a follow-up: influence for *whom* exactly? Is it enough if some community members have power over decisions, or does it matter who those people are?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is on the right track but second-guessing themselves. I should validate the core insight while pushing them to think about the complications — because measuring participation isn't just about degree of influence, there are also questions about who participates, how representative they are, etc. This will enrich their reflection without giving them the full answer.

exchanges.student: oh wait yeah, that matters a lot lol. if it’s only local elites or men or ppl already connected, then it looks participatory but isn’t really broad participation.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly — so you now have two dimensions for measuring participation: depth of influence and breadth of inclusion. Keep those in mind. Now, let's tackle the tension you mentioned. Do you see a difference between using participation as a *method* to get better development outcomes, versus participation as a *goal* in itself?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now identified both dimensions of measuring participation: depth (degree of influence) and breadth (who is included). This is excellent progress. I should affirm this and now pivot toward the goal vs. method tension, which they originally said they were stuck on. I'll connect what they've discovered to that tension to help them see the link.

exchanges.student: yeah i think so, but i’m a lil fuzzy. method = participation helps make projects work better, and goal = participation is valuable even if it doesn’t make outcomes better?
exchanges.tutor: Good, that's the right distinction. Now think about a concrete situation: what happens when genuine community participation slows down a project, or leads to decisions that experts think are suboptimal? Which value wins — the method or the goal? What does that conflict reveal?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has captured the basic distinction correctly. Now I should push them to think about why these two framings might create tension or conflict in practice, rather than just defining them. This moves toward the 'can the tension be resolved' part of the prompt.

exchanges.student: ugh yeah that’s the part messing me up. i think it reveals that participation can be treated as legit only when it helps efficiency, which kinda undermines it as empowerment.
exchanges.tutor: That's a sharp observation. So given that tension, what do you think — is it actually resolvable? Or is it a fundamental contradiction that planners just have to manage?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has articulated the core tension really well — participation gets instrumentalized when treated purely as a method, which undercuts its value as empowerment. This is a strong insight. Now I should push them toward the resolution question in the prompt: can this tension be resolved, and if so, how? I want them to think this through themselves rather than just restating the problem.

exchanges.student: i’m leaning that it’s not fully resolvable tbh, but maybe it can be managed if planners are honest abt tradeoffs and give communities real decision power instead of fake consultation.
exchanges.tutor: That's a defensible position — hold onto it for your paper. Now there's one big part of the prompt you haven't touched yet: what *structural factors* made participation rise to prominence as a development strategy in the first place? Why did it become such a big deal? What do you think drove that shift?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has taken a clear position with a reasonable argument. This is good — they're not just describing the problem but engaging with it normatively. I should affirm this and now redirect to the last major part of the prompt they haven't addressed yet: the structural factors that made participation such a key element in development strategies. We've spent enough time on the tension and I want to make sure they cover the full prompt in their reflection.

exchanges.student: maybe bc top-down development kept failing? and also ppl started criticizing states/experts for being too centralized or disconnected, but i’m not sure what else to include.
exchanges.tutor: Good start. Think bigger picture: were there any major shifts in global politics or economics around the 1980s-90s that changed how development was approached? And who were the new actors pushing for participation beyond just states?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified one key structural factor (failure of top-down approaches) but is unsure what else to include. I should push them to think more broadly without giving examples directly. I can prompt them to think about the global/historical context — like the role of NGOs, international institutions, colonial legacies, or the political shifts of the 1980s-90s — without naming those for them.

exchanges.student: maybe neoliberal stuff? like the state got criticized, NGOs and donors got bigger, and participation became a way to make projects seem more legit?
exchanges.tutor: Yes, that's a strong connection. You've now got the building blocks: the spectrum of participation, how to measure it, the goal/method tension and your take on it, and the structural factors. That's enough to write your reflection. Try drafting it — what's your opening argument going to be?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has connected neoliberalism, the rise of NGOs/donors, and legitimacy — which are strong structural factors. They've now covered enough ground to attempt a written draft. We've had a productive 9-exchange conversation covering: methods of participation, measuring participation (depth and breadth), the goal vs. method tension, and structural factors. It's time to push them toward integrating all of this into their actual reflection piece, which is the assignment.
